q: Uniform circular motion occurs when an object moves in a circle at a constant speed.
In uniform circular motion, the magnitude of velocity changes continuously.
The continuous change in the direction of velocity in circular motion does not constitute an acceleration.
An object revolving in a circle at constant speed is still continuously accelerating.
Average acceleration is defined as the change in velocity vector divided by the time interval.
Instantaneous acceleration can be obtained when the time interval $\Delta t$ approaches zero.
In uniform circular motion, the change in the velocity vector $\Delta \vec{v}$ points away from the center of the circle.
Centripetal acceleration is also known as radial acceleration because it is directed along the radius towards the center.
The centripetal acceleration $a_c$ is directly proportional to the radius $r$ and the square of the linear speed $v$.
The equation $a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}$ expresses centripetal acceleration in terms of linear speed and radius.
Substituting $v = r \omega$ into the centripetal acceleration formula yields $a_c = r \omega^2$.
In uniform circular motion, the centripetal acceleration vector is tangential to the path.
The linear velocity vector in uniform circular motion is perpendicular to the circular path.
Centripetal acceleration and linear velocity are perpendicular to each other at every point in the path.
Nonuniform circular motion occurs when an object moves in a circular path with a varying speed.
In nonuniform circular motion, the angular speed of the object remains constant.
Angular acceleration gives rise to tangential acceleration in nonuniform circular motion.
In nonuniform circular motion, only centripetal acceleration is present.
The resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion is the scalar sum of centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The two acceleration vectors, $\vec{a_c}$ and $\vec{a_t}$, are always parallel to each other.
The centripetal acceleration vector $\vec{a_c}$ is in the tangential direction.
The tangential acceleration vector $\vec{a_t}$ is in the radial direction.
The magnitude of resultant acceleration $a$ is given by $a = \sqrt{a_c^2 + a_t^2}$.
The direction of resultant acceleration $\vec{a}$ is determined by $\tan \phi = \frac{a_c}{a_t}$.
Centripetal acceleration is present only in nonuniform circular motion.
Tangential acceleration is present in both uniform and nonuniform circular motion.
If $\Delta t$ approaches zero, $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ also become very small.
When $\Delta t$ approaches zero, $\vec{v_2}$ will be almost perpendicular to $\vec{v_1}$.
The acceleration vector $\vec{a}$ points toward the center of the circle in uniform circular motion.
The limit of $\frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ as $\Delta t \to 0$ represents the linear speed $v$.
The centripetal acceleration $a_c = r \omega^2$ is only valid when the angular speed $\omega$ is constant.
The resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion is always directed towards the center of the circle.
The angular acceleration in nonuniform circular motion gives rise to the centripetal acceleration.
If an object is in nonuniform circular motion, its tangential acceleration will be zero.
The direction of angular velocity can be specified using the right-hand rule.
The centripetal acceleration acts along the radius and points radially outward from the center of the circle.
The magnitude of the resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion can be zero if $a_c$ and $a_t$ cancel each other out.
The relation $\tan \phi = \frac{a_t}{a_c}$ provides the angle of the resultant acceleration vector relative to the centripetal acceleration vector.
In a situation where an object's speed is constant, the only acceleration present is centripetal acceleration.
For an object to have tangential acceleration, its angular speed must be changing.
